questions.question: Which WCAG principle states that users must be able to operate all interface components?
questions.explanation: Operable means users can interact with all functionality. This includes keyboard accessibility, enough time to complete tasks, avoiding seizure-inducing content, and clear navigation.

questions.question: Which element should be used for a clickable action that submits a form?
questions.explanation: Buttons provide built-in keyboard support, proper semantics, and accessibility APIs. Developers often misuse links or divs for actions.

questions.question: Which statement about ARIA is TRUE?
options: ARIA improves accessibility in every situation.
ARIA should replace semantic HTML whenever possible.
No ARIA is better than bad ARIA.
questions.correctAnswer: No ARIA is better than bad ARIA.
questions.explanation: Improper ARIA frequently creates accessibility barriers. Native HTML should always be the first choice.

questions.question: Which keyboard interaction is expected for a native button?
questions.explanation: Native buttons activate using both Enter and Space. Custom controls should replicate this behavior.

questions.question: A modal dialog opens. What should happen immediately?
options: Focus should remain on the triggering button.
Focus should move into the dialog.
Focus should move to the browser address bar.
questions.correctAnswer: Focus should move into the dialog.
questions.explanation: Users navigating with keyboards or assistive technologies should immediately interact with the dialog without tabbing through the underlying page.

questions.question: When a modal dialog closes, where should keyboard focus generally return?
options: The element that opened the dialog
questions.correctAnswer: The element that opened the dialog
questions.explanation: Returning focus maintains context and prevents keyboard users from becoming lost.

questions.question: Which implementation correctly associates a label with an input?
questions.explanation: Explicit label associations provide the most reliable support across browsers and assistive technologies.

questions.question: A purely decorative image should use which alt attribute?
questions.explanation: Decorative images should have empty alt text so screen readers ignore them.

questions.question: Which source has the highest priority when computing an element's accessible name?
questions.explanation: The Accessible Name and Description Computation gives aria-labelledby the highest precedence.

questions.question: According to WCAG AA, what is the minimum contrast ratio required for normal text?
questions.explanation: Normal text requires at least 4.5:1 at Level AA. Large text requires 3:1.

questions.question: Large text under WCAG AA requires a minimum contrast ratio of:
questions.explanation: Large text is at least 18pt (or 14pt bold) and may use a 3:1 ratio.

questions.question: Why is heading hierarchy important?
options: Allows screen reader users to navigate page structure efficiently
questions.correctAnswer: Allows screen reader users to navigate page structure efficiently
questions.explanation: Screen reader users frequently navigate by headings to understand document organization.

questions.question: Which table element identifies column headers?
questions.explanation: Header cells should use <th>, often with scope attributes.

questions.question: Which ARIA attribute announces changing content without moving keyboard focus?
questions.explanation: Live regions notify assistive technologies when dynamic content changes.

questions.question: Which new WCAG 2.2 requirement helps ensure interactive controls are easier to activate on touch devices?
questions.explanation: WCAG 2.2 introduced minimum target size requirements to improve usability for users with limited dexterity.
